chunk_summary: This section discusses trajectory-based growth faltering thresholds, emphasizing growth patterns over single measures, and provides escalation guidelines for malnutrition risk in infants and young children.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of growth trajectory monitoring over single z-score cutoffs in identifying malnutrition risk.
Learn the key referral triggers for growth faltering in infants based on Indian growth monitoring guidelines.
Familiarize with WHO weight-velocity standards and bedside heuristics for assessing growth adequacy.
Recognize danger signs that necessitate urgent escalation in cases of growth faltering.
key_facts: Single-measure cutoffs like WAZ < -2 SD may miss high-risk children deviating from their growth trajectory.
Referral triggers include crossing down two major percentile lines, weight loss, or lack of weight gain for 1 month (0–6 months) or 2–3 months (6–12 months).
WHO weight-velocity standards provide expected weight increments over defined intervals for age-based assessment.
Term infants typically regain birth weight by 10–14 days and gain 25–30 g/day until 3 months, with slower gain thereafter.
Escalation is recommended when growth faltering coexists with danger signs like dehydration, respiratory distress, or suspected serious infection.
reference_quote: The WHO weight-velocity standards provide expected weight increments over defined intervals and can be used to judge whether recent gain is within the expected range for age. (Source: https://www.who.int/tools/childgrowth-standards/standards/weight-velocity)
document_key_topics: Growth faltering thresholds and trajectory monitoring
WHO weight-velocity standards and bedside heuristics
